rows.v: Two opposite prefixes: 'hyper-' means excessive or above normal; 'hypo-' means deficient or below normal. They attach to countless terms to flag too much or too little of something.
The patient was hypertensive on arrival (high blood pressure) but became hypotensive after the bleed, her pressure dropping dangerously low.
From Greek 'hyper' (over, above) and 'hypo' (under, beneath). The same roots give us everyday words like 'hyperactive' and 'hypothermia'.
A suffix meaning inflammation. Attach it to a body part and you have the condition of that part being inflamed — one of the most common building blocks in all of medicine.
Appendicitis, tonsillitis, arthritis, bronchitis — once you know '-itis' means inflammation, half the chart starts to decode itself.
From Greek, originally an adjective ending meaning 'pertaining to'. It came to specifically denote inflammatory disease in medical Latin.
A suffix meaning surgical removal of something. Attach it to an organ or structure and it names the operation to take that thing out.
She underwent an appendectomy (removal of the appendix); a hysterectomy removes the uterus, a mastectomy the breast.
From Greek 'ektomē' (a cutting out), from 'ek' (out) + 'temnein' (to cut). The same 'temnein' root runs through much of surgical vocabulary.
'-otomy' means cutting into (an incision); '-ostomy' means creating a permanent opening. One cuts in; the other makes a lasting mouth or passage.
A tracheotomy is an incision into the windpipe; a colostomy creates a permanent opening from the colon to the abdominal wall.
Both from Greek: '-otomy' from 'temnein' (to cut), '-ostomy' from 'stoma' (mouth, opening). 'Stoma' is still used alone for the opening itself.
A suffix meaning the study or science of something. It names both fields of medicine and the specialists who practice them (via '-ologist').
Cardiology is the study of the heart, so a cardiologist treats it; neurology, the nervous system; oncology, cancer.
From Greek 'logos' (word, study, reason). It's one of the most productive suffixes in all of English, far beyond medicine.
A suffix referring to a condition of the blood. It signals that the root word describes something about the blood's state or contents.
Anemia is a deficiency of healthy red blood cells; hypoglycemia is low blood sugar; leukemia is a cancer of the blood-forming tissues.
From Greek 'haima' (blood) — the same root behind 'hemoglobin' and 'hemorrhage'. Spelled '-aemia' in British usage.
A suffix meaning pain. Attach it to a body part and it names pain in that location.
Neuralgia is nerve pain; myalgia, muscle pain; arthralgia, joint pain. Fibromyalgia combines fiber, muscle, and pain.
From Greek 'algos' (pain). The same root gives 'analgesic' — literally a substance that takes pain away.
Two opposite prefixes for speed: 'brady-' means slow; 'tachy-' means fast. Most often applied to heart rate and breathing.
Bradycardia is a slow heart rate; tachycardia, a fast one. A monitor alarm for 'tachy' means the heart is racing.
From Greek 'bradys' (slow) and 'tachys' (swift) — the latter also behind 'tachometer', the gauge measuring engine speed.
Directional terms: 'anterior' means toward the front of the body; 'posterior' means toward the back. They orient where a structure or injury is.
The anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) sits at the front of the knee; a posterior approach enters from the back.
From Latin 'ante' (before) and 'post' (after, behind). 'Ventral' (belly) and 'dorsal' (back) are near-synonyms, especially in anatomy.
Directional terms relative to the body's center: 'proximal' means closer to the trunk or point of origin; 'distal' means farther away.
The wrist is distal to the elbow; the shoulder is proximal. A distal fracture of the radius is near the wrist end of the bone.
From Latin 'proximus' (nearest) and 'distare' (to stand apart) — the same roots behind 'proximity' and 'distant'.
Body positions: 'supine' is lying face-up on the back; 'prone' is lying face-down. Critical for exams, surgery, and positioning patients.
Place the patient supine for the abdominal exam; prone positioning is used to improve oxygenation in some respiratory failure.
From Latin 'supinus' (lying on the back) and 'pronus' (bent forward). A memory aid: 'supine' contains 'up' — face up.
Directional terms: 'lateral' means toward the side, away from the midline; 'medial' means toward the midline of the body.
The little toe is on the lateral side of the foot; the big toe is medial. A lateral ankle sprain affects the outer ligaments.
From Latin 'latus' (side) and 'medius' (middle). 'Bilateral' (both sides) and 'median' share these roots.
A combining form meaning heart. It opens a huge family of terms about the heart and circulation.
Cardiology, cardiac arrest, cardiomyopathy, electrocardiogram (ECG/EKG) — all built on the heart root.
From Greek 'kardia' (heart). The same root underlies 'cardiac' and, more distantly, the idea of the heart as the body's core.
A combining form meaning skin. It anchors the vocabulary of skin health and disease.
Dermatology is the study of skin; dermatitis is skin inflammation; the dermis is the layer beneath the surface epidermis.
From Greek 'derma' (skin). 'Epidermis' literally means 'upon the skin' — the outermost layer.
A combining form meaning stomach, and by extension the digestive tract.
Gastritis is stomach inflammation; gastroenteritis adds the intestines; a gastroenterologist treats the whole digestive system.
From Greek 'gastēr' (stomach, belly). The same root appears in 'gastronomy' — the art and science of good eating.
Two ways to describe a condition's timeline: 'acute' means sudden onset and short duration; 'chronic' means long-lasting or persistent.
An acute asthma attack comes on fast and resolves; chronic asthma is an ongoing condition managed for years.
From Latin 'acutus' (sharp, pointed) and Greek 'chronos' (time). 'Chronic' literally means 'of time' — defined by its duration.
Swelling caused by excess fluid trapped in the body's tissues. A common, visible sign with many possible causes.
The patient had pitting edema in both ankles — pressing a finger left a dent — pointing the team toward a heart or kidney workup.
From Greek 'oidēma' (a swelling). Spelled 'oedema' in British usage. The related 'edematous' describes the swollen tissue.
The medical term for fainting — a temporary loss of consciousness from a brief drop in blood flow to the brain.
She experienced syncope after standing too quickly; the episode was brief, and she recovered fully within a minute.
From Greek 'synkopē' (a cutting short) — also, fittingly, a music term for an interrupted rhythm. Pronounced 'SIN-ko-pee'.
Difficult or labored breathing; the sensation of shortness of breath. One of the most important symptoms to assess.
The patient reported dyspnea on exertion — breathless after climbing one flight of stairs — prompting a cardiac and pulmonary evaluation.
From Greek 'dys-' (difficult, bad) + 'pnoia' (breathing). The 'dys-' prefix recurs everywhere: dysfunction, dysphagia, dysuria.
'Febrile' means having a fever; 'afebrile' means without fever. Basic but constant vocabulary in any clinical setting.
The child arrived febrile at 103°F; by morning she was afebrile and alert, and the team relaxed.
From Latin 'febris' (fever) — the same root behind 'fever' itself. The 'a-' prefix in 'afebrile' means 'without'.
A state of drowsiness, sluggishness, and reduced alertness. A subjective but clinically meaningful sign of how sick someone is.
The normally energetic toddler was lethargic and hard to rouse, a red flag that escalated the urgency of the visit.
From Greek 'lēthargia' (drowsiness), linked to 'Lethe', the mythological river of forgetfulness in the underworld.
Describes a disease or condition with no known cause. A precise, honest label for 'we don't know why this is happening'.
After every test came back normal, the diagnosis was idiopathic — the cause of her condition simply couldn't be identified.
From Greek 'idios' (one's own, peculiar) + 'pathos' (suffering, disease). One wit defined it as 'idiot doctors, pathetic patients' — a joke, not the etymology.
Affecting both sides of the body. Its opposite, 'unilateral', means one side only.
Bilateral pneumonia means both lungs are involved; a unilateral weakness raises concern for a stroke on one side of the brain.
From Latin 'bi-' (two) + 'latus' (side). The same 'latus' root behind 'lateral' and 'medial'.
Removing a small sample of tissue to examine it, usually to diagnose or rule out disease such as cancer.
The suspicious mole was removed and sent for biopsy; the pathology report confirmed it was benign.
From Greek 'bios' (life) + 'opsis' (sight) — literally 'viewing of living tissue'. A 'necropsy' (autopsy) views the dead instead.
Complete Blood Count — one of the most common lab tests, measuring red cells, white cells, and platelets to give a broad snapshot of health.
Her CBC showed a high white-cell count, supporting the suspicion of an infection driving the fever.
A modern clinical abbreviation. Each component (hemoglobin, hematocrit, WBC) carries its own roots, but 'CBC' itself is plain initials.
Two imaging tests: MRI (Magnetic Resonance Imaging) uses magnets and radio waves for detailed soft-tissue pictures; CT (Computed Tomography) uses X-rays for fast cross-sectional views.
The CT scan was ordered first to rule out a bleed quickly; an MRI followed for a finer look at the soft tissue.
MRI is an initialism for its physics; CT comes from Greek 'tomos' (a slice, a cut) + 'graphein' (to write) — imaging by slices.
Inserting a tube into the airway (the trachea) to keep it open and allow mechanical breathing support.
As the patient's breathing failed, the team moved to intubation, securing the airway before connecting the ventilator.
From Latin 'in' (into) + 'tuba' (tube). The reverse procedure, removing the tube, is 'extubation'.
A thin, flexible tube inserted into the body to drain fluid, deliver medication, or access a vessel. The term covers many types.
A urinary catheter was placed to monitor output; a central catheter gave direct access to a large vein for medications.
From Greek 'kathetēr' (something let down or inserted), from 'kathienai' (to send down). One of the oldest words in surgical practice.
An order meaning 'nothing by mouth' — no food or drink, usually before surgery or certain tests to keep the stomach empty.
The patient was kept NPO after midnight in preparation for the morning operation.
From Latin 'nil per os' (nothing through the mouth). One of many Latin abbreviations that survive in modern medication and nursing orders.
Intravenous — into or within a vein. Refers to the line, the access, and the route for delivering fluids and medications directly into the bloodstream.
They started an IV to deliver fluids and antibiotics; 'IV push' means giving a medication directly and quickly through that line.
From Latin 'intra' (within) + 'vena' (vein). The contrast 'IM' is intramuscular — into the muscle instead.
An order meaning immediately, without delay. One of the most urgent words in a hospital.
'Get me a STAT chest X-ray' means now — the order jumps to the front of the line.
From Latin 'statim' (immediately, at once). One of the most recognizable bits of medical shorthand, even outside the field.
An instruction meaning 'as needed' — a medication or action to be done when required rather than on a fixed schedule.
Pain medication was ordered PRN, so the patient received it only when the pain returned, not by the clock.
From Latin 'pro re nata' (literally 'for the thing born', i.e. as the situation arises). A staple of prescription writing.
Dosing frequencies: 'BID' means twice a day; 'TID' means three times a day. 'QID' (four times) and 'QD' (once daily) round out the family.
The antibiotic was prescribed BID — one tablet morning and evening — for ten days.
From Latin 'bis in die' (twice a day) and 'ter in die' (three times a day). These Latin dosing codes remain standard on prescriptions.
Common medical shorthand: 'Rx' means prescription or treatment; 'Dx' means diagnosis. 'Sx' (symptoms) and 'Hx' (history) complete the set.
The chart listed the Dx as pneumonia and the Rx as a course of antibiotics plus rest.
'Rx' is often traced to the Latin 'recipe' (take thou), or to the symbol of the Roman god Jupiter; 'Dx', 'Sx', 'Hx' use 'x' as a general abbreviation marker.
Short for vital signs — the core measurements of body function: temperature, pulse, respiratory rate, blood pressure, and often oxygen saturation.
'Vitals are stable' is shorthand for reassurance; a sudden change in vitals is often the first warning of trouble.
From Latin 'vitalis' (of life), from 'vita' (life). They're called 'vital' because they signal whether the body's basic life functions are holding.
'Hx' means history — most often the patient's medical background. 'PMH' is past medical history; 'FHx' is family history.
Her Hx included asthma and a prior surgery; the PMH guided which medications were safe to give.
'Hx' uses 'H' for history plus 'x' as the standard abbreviation marker, matching Dx, Sx, and Rx in clinical shorthand.
